agent1_snippets.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Iron Therapies
agent1_snippets.content: HIF – prolyl hydroxylase inhibitors (HIF – PHIs) are a novel class of therapeutic agents that raise erythropoietin levels by stabilizing HIF levels, thereby increasing endogenous erythropoietin production. HIF–PHIs also decrease hepcidin levels. In 2023, daprodustat was approved by the FDA for use in patients on dialysis for longer than 4 months. This compound is currently not approved for use in non-dialysis patients. [62] [63] [62]

agent1_snippets.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: Newer and potentially less expensive ESA alternatives include epoetin alfa-epbx, a genetically engineered recombinant human erythropoietin, and CERA. HIF – PHIs are a novel class of therapeutic agents that raise erythropoietin levels by stabilizing HIF, which in turn boosts endogenous erythropoietin production. Currently, daprodustat is the only FDA-approved medication in this class. Several other new agents are available, including ferric pyrophosphate (delivered through dialysate), ferric citrate (which also acts as an oral phosphate binder), ziltivekimab (an anti-inflammatory antibody), and ferric maltol/sucrosomial iron (which bypasses stomach absorption and minimizes gastrointestinal effects). Blood transfusions should be avoided as much as possible, especially for patients who may be renal transplant candidates, to prevent allosensitization.

agent1_snippets.content: The FDA has rejected several other drugs in this class, which are still currently used in other countries. This class of medication can be taken orally rather than IV or SC. Preliminary evidence comparing ESAs to HIF–PHIs suggests the possibility of increased cardiovascular events in non-dialysis-dependent CKD patients (but not dialysis-dependent patients). [48] These medications are thought to have similar adverse effects to ESAs, including the risk of perpetuating malignant cells. [59] [64] In addition, HIF is thought to contribute to angiogenesis, which could worsen conditions such as retinopathy. The HIF activation pathways also contribute to cyst formation in polycystic kidney disease, and effects on cyst growth are also unknown. So far, data has not shown an increased risk above ESAs, but given the relative newness of this class of medications, the full effects may not yet be known. [2] [64]

agent1_snippets.content: Emerging treatments include luspatercept, an agent that stimulates late-stage erythropoiesis. agent1_snippets.title: Luspatercept -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Luspatercept is also indicated for patients who fail ESA therapy and fall into one of the following scenarios: 1) require 2 or more red blood cell units over 8 weeks with very low-to-intermediate risk myelodysplastic syndromes with ring sideroblasts (MDS-RS), or 2) have a myelodysplastic/myeloproliferative neoplasm with ring sideroblasts and thrombocytosis (MDS/MPN-RS-T). The ringed sideroblastic phenotype subgroup of patients typically demonstrates a higher response rate. [5] Refractory anemia with ring sideroblasts (RARS) and iron overload are complications managed similarly to low-risk MDS and MPN. Patients with MDS and an SF3B1 mutation demonstrate a homogeneous disease phenotype characterized by dysplasia of granulocytic or megakaryocytic lineages, suggesting that the SF3B1 region significantly affects the severity or subtype of MDS. agent1_snippets.title: Anemia -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: 4) Anemia due to chronic disease: Anemia in the setting of renal failure, responds to erythropoietin. Autoimmune and rheumatological conditions causing anemia require treatment of the underlying disease. 5) Anemia due to increased red blood cell destruction: Hemolytic anemia caused by faulty mechanical valves will need replacement. Hemolytic anemia due to medications requires the removal of the offending drug. Persistent hemolytic anemia requires splenectomy. Hemoglobinopathies such as sickle anemia require blood transfusions, exchange transfusions, and even hydroxyurea to decrease the incidence of sickling. DIC, which is characterized by uncontrolled coagulation and thrombosis, requires the removal of the offending stimulus. Patients with life-threatening bleeding require the use of antifibrinolytic agents.

agent1_snippets.title: Sideroblastic Anemia -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Symptomatic sideroblastic anemia in MDS/MPN variants is treated similarly to low-risk MDS. Therapies previously used include erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF), and hypomethylating agents (HMAs) such as azacytidine and decitabine, all of which are potential options to reduce the need for red blood cell transfusions. [34] Though some patients may be successful on the watch-and-wait regimen, HMAs are frequently used after the failure of conservative management with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents and G-CSF. Administration of 75 mg/m 2 azacitidine for 7 consecutive days with a 28-day cycle subcutaneously or intravenously is an option for patients older than 20 who meet the criteria for RARS and whose neutrophil counts are less than 1x10 9 /L.

agent2_snippets.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
agent2_snippets.content: Antianemic preparations belong to the larger class of blood‑ and blood‑forming organ drugs and are grouped under the ATC category “other hematological agents.” This therapeutic group includes vitamin B12 formulations (cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin) and folic‑acid preparations, as well as iron‑based antianemic agents that form a distinct subclass. Iron‑based agents comprise bivalent oral iron compounds such as iron ascorbate (askorbát železnatý), trivalent oral iron compounds such as ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate, and iron‑folic‑acid combinations exemplified by ferrous amino‑acid chelate. Iron compounds are also classified as “železité sloučeniny,” a subordinate group within organometallic compounds (“organokovové sloučeniny”). Calcium phosphate is categorized as an industrial aid used in manufacturing, while mercurial diuretics and xanthine‑derived low‑ceiling diuretics are placed together under “types and miscellaneous diuretics” as low‑ceiling agents distinct from thiazides. The “other hematological agents” ATC group further encompasses heme‑based pharmacologic substances, hereditary‑angioedema treatments (ecallantide and icatibant), hemostatic agents such as vitamin K, and the sickle‑cell disease drug voxelotor. Overall, the taxonomy links antianemic and iron‑based therapies with broader hematological drug classes, situating vitamin B12/folic‑acid formulations, various iron compounds, diuretics, industrial aids, hemostatics, heme products, hereditary‑angioedema agents, and voxelotor within a unified framework that supports comprehensive patient care and drug classification.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 728
agent2_snippets.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS represent a broad therapeutic category used to treat various forms of anemia. Within this category, iron antianemic preparations constitute a major subclass, indicating that formulations containing iron are a specific type of antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are further divided into distinct groups: Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations are oral formulations that provide bivalent (Fe²⁺) iron, and Iron in combination with folic acid includes products that pair iron with folic acid to enhance hematologic response. Both of these groups are classified as types of iron antianemic preparations. ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS also encompass preparations based on folic acid or vitamin B12, reflecting the clinical practice of using these vitamins either alone or in combination with iron to correct deficiencies that contribute to anemia. Specific compounds illustrate the detailed composition of these subgroups. FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE is identified as a member of the Iron in combination with folic acid group, indicating that this chelated iron‑amino acid complex is formulated together with folic acid. Likewise, askorbát železnatý (iron ascorbate) is a member of the Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations, showing that this iron‑ascorbate compound is used in oral bivalent iron formulations. Together, these relationships clarify how antianemic therapy is organized: a general class (ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS) includes iron‑based products (iron antianemic preparations), which are further specialized by route, iron valence, and adjunctive nutrients such as folic acid, vitamin B12, or ascorbate, with specific agents like FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE and askorbát železnatý representing concrete examples within these subcategories.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
agent2_snippets.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS constitute a therapeutic class that is defined by the presence of folic acid and vitamin B12 components. Within this class, agents that combine folic acid and vitamin B12 are recognized as a specific subgroup. This subgroup is itself a type of the broader “vitamin B12 and folic acid” category, indicating that antianemic preparations are fundamentally linked to these essential nutrients. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and its analogues) is classified as a member of the “vitamin B12 and folic acid” group and further comprises individual forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin. These specific analogues represent distinct chemical entities that share the core activity of vitamin B12 supplementation. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 335
agent2_snippets.content: Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations and Iron in other combinations are both specific subclasses of iron antianemic preparations, indicating that they are oral formulations intended to treat or prevent iron deficiency. Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations is a member of the group askorbát železnatý, signifying that it contains iron in the form of iron‑ascorbate. Iron in other combinations includes the product FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITAMS, which is classified as a multivitamin preparation that combines iron with folic acid and a broad spectrum of additional nutrients. FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITAMS has two detailed ingredient listings. One listing enumerates a complex blend of active substances: LEUCOVORIN 2.5 mg, folic acid 1 mg, LEVOMEFOLATE MAGNESIUM 7 mg, FERROUS CYSTEINE GLYCINATE 13.6 mg, several phospholipid derivatives (1,2‑DOCOSAHEXANOYL‑SN‑GLYCERO‑3‑PHOSPHOSERINE CALCIUM 6.4 mg, 1,2‑ICOSAPENTOYL‑SN‑GLYCERO‑3‑PHOSPHOSERINE CALCIUM 800 µg, PHOSPHATIDYL SERINE 12 mg), PYRIDOXAL PHOSPHATE ANHYDROUS 25 µg, FLAVIN ADENINE DINUCLEOTIDE 25 µg, NADH 25 µg, COBAMAMIDE 50 µg, COCARBOXYLASE 25 µg, MAGNESIUM ASCORBATE 24 mg, ZINC ASCORBATE 1 mg, MAGNESIUM L‑THREONATE 1 mg, BETAINE 500 µg, CITRIC ACID MONOHYDRATE 1.83 mg and SODIUM CITRATE 3.67 mg, delivered in delayed‑release oral capsules. The second listing specifies a simpler composition of ascorbic acid 40 mg, ferrous fumarate 62.5 mg, folic acid 1 mg, niacin 3 mg and polysaccharide iron complex 62.5 mg in an oral capsule. These relationships illustrate that iron antianemic preparations can be formulated in various oral dosage forms, often incorporating vitamin C (ascorbic acid) to enhance iron absorption and folic acid to support hematopoiesis. The inclusion of multiple vitamins, minerals, and co‑factors within a single multivitamin product reflects a therapeutic strategy aimed at correcting iron deficiency anemia while simultaneously addressing broader nutritional needs.
